issue_title: UTM zone exceptions error
issue_body:
By definition zones are left-closed, right-open intervals, e.g. zone 31: 0 <= latitude < 6.
In function latlon_to_zone_number:

```
if 72 <= latitude <= 84 and longitude >= 0:
    if longitude <= 9:
        return 31
    elif longitude <= 21:
        return 33
    elif longitude <= 33:
        return 35
    elif longitude <= 42:
        return 37
```

For latitudes >=72, this results in:
zone 31: 0  <= longitude <= 9
zone 33: 9  <   longitude <= 21
zone 35: 21<   longitude <= 33
zone 37: 33<   longitude <= 42

but for latitudes < 72:
zone 37: 36 <= latitude < 42

issue_title: ureg.check fails to check dimensionless quantity
issue_body:
Hello,

Thank you for this useful package.

I think there is an issue with the quantity type checking decorator. It doesn't raise any exception when the passed quantity is not dimensionless as expected.

Consider the following example:
```
import pint
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
@ureg.check(ureg(''))
def f(x):
    return 2*x
print(f(ureg.Quantity(1, '')))
print(f(ureg.Quantity(1, 'm')))
```
The first call to "f" is correct, the second one should raise an exception but it doesn't happen.

Thanks to take some time to analyze this issue.

issue_title: Implement Transfomer.echos()
Issue in blockfeeder?
issue_body:
When using your blockfeeder I frequently get an "invalid padding byte" on the last decrypter.feed() call to pick up the last decrypted block. I'm not an expert on AES or any of this code, but I think there is a bug here in the strip_PKCS7_padding method where it always treats a block as having padding when it may not (i.e. if the final decrypt resulted in a block of 16 bytes). Specifically, [here](https://github.com/ricmoo/pyaes/blob/master/pyaes/util.py#L57)

I think this should just return the data if the final byte is > chr(16)? I may be completely off on that though?

issue_title: V2 tokens expire after 1 year.
issue_body:
The token for one of the very first will instances (maybe the oldest living) just expired, rather unexpectedly.  Everyone else's installs will also be expiring then, as time passes - it turns out hipchat has a (somewhat undocumented) one-year expiration on all V2 tokens.

I have an open issue with Hipchat on this, and am hoping to have a long-term fix so this never comes up again.

The short-term fix is to regenerate will's personal token, and update all of your instances and environments (I know, this is a complete pain. If you're not yet expired, I'd say wait for a more permanent solution.)

I'll keep this issue updated with information as I have it.

issue_title: Segmentation Fault with Operational HRRR files
issue_body:
Hello,

I have recently discovered that pygrib 1.9.8 is unable to read GRIB2 files that the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) provides for the operational HRRR model. These files are available at  http://www.ftp.ncep.noaa.gov/data/nccf/nonoperational/com/hrrr/prod/. Here is some example code:

    >>> import pygrib
    >>> igrb = pygrib.open('hrrr.t00z.wrfsfcf05.grib2')
    >>> rr = igrb.message(1)
    >>> rr
    1:Maximum/Composite radar reflectivity:dB (instant):lambert:unknown:level 0 10:fcst time 5 hrs:from
    201511050000
    >>> rr_array = rr.values
    Segmentation fault (core dumped)

The file can be opened and a message read in, but any attempt to read the message data array using `.values` or `.data` results in a segmentation fault.

Further investigation suggests that the problem may be with the packing scheme used in these files. They use complex packing and spatial differencing (scheme 3 in grib_repack). If I use the wgrib2 utility to repack a file with another scheme (e.g., simple packing),  pygrib can read the file with no errors. Additionally, if I attempt to repackage a message using code similar to what is in grib_repack, I get the following errors:

    >>> rr['editionNumber']
    2
    >>> rr['dataRepresentationTemplateNumber']
    3
    >>> rr['dataRepresentationTemplateNumber'] = 0

    RuntimeError: b'Key/value not found'

This looks to me like pygrib is looking for some packing parameters that do not exist in the message.

This problem is occurring on a 64-bit Ubuntu linux machine. Has anyone else encountered this problem or have an explanation of what is different about these files?

Regards,

issue_title: Backends must not reorder source files
issue_body:
Currently the Vivado backends (and others as well from a quick look) don't place HDL sources into the project file in the order they are resolved from the dependency resolver, but reorder the files. The Vivado backend, for example, groups the HDL files by file type (first all verilog files, then all sv files, then all vhdl files).

For most Verilog and VHDL files, the compile order doesn't matter.  However, sometimes it does matter -- for example, for package definitions in SystemVerilog. The SV standard requires that packages are defined before they are used, hence these files must be compiled first.

Note: Vivado doesn't seem to care about that rule and still compiles the resulting project without errors. However, SpyGlass isn't as lenient and fails the lint process. However, let's make sure we don't rely on lenient behavior by tools but do the right thing (TM).

TODOs:
- [ ] create testcases which check this behavior
- [ ] fix all backends showing to be problematic. Vivado is one of them.

issue_title: ProxyResolver can't find the correct proxy for URL with port number
issue_body:
I think there is a bug in https://github.com/carsonyl/pypac/blob/master/pypac/resolver.py#L51 and https://github.com/carsonyl/pypac/blob/master/pypac/resolver.py#L55:

https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.parse.html#urllib.parse.urlparse is used to parse the URL. Then the ``.netloc`` part is used as host when calling ``FindProxyForURL`` to determine the proxy.

BUT the ``.netloc`` part also contains the ``:`` and the port number and not just the pure host name.

In contrast to that http://findproxyforurl.com/netscape-documentation/ states:
> host
>     the hostname extracted from the URL. This is only for convenience, it is the exact same string as between :// and the first : or / after that. The port number is not included in this parameter. It can be extracted from the URL when necessary.

So splitting before the ``:`` would help to resolve the correct host if there is a port number in the URL.

issue_title: Python install setup.py parametr
issue_body:
When installing through `setup.py`, it selects the wrong host `x86`.
When installing via `cMake`, this is treated ` -T host = x64`
How to pass this parameter through `setup.py`?

issue_title: Proposal: ignore bmp 'Reserved Area' value
issue_body:
The header of .bmp images has 4 bytes starting from the 0x06 address marked as  ['Reserved'](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BMP_file_format#Bitmap_file_header), most bmp images I've come across have these 4 bytes zeroed, however that isn't the case for all images, as some softwares uses this reserved areas for their purposes.

In my opinion these bytes must be ignored (every software I've used to open bmp images ignores it).

Relevant lines:
https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/996a4bbba51381d21a8893e51a7da31587a9c618/dlib/image_loader/image_loader.h#L82-L84
https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/996a4bbba51381d21a8893e51a7da31587a9c618/dlib/image_loader/image_loader.h#L92-L93

* **Version**: 996a4bbba51381d21a8893e51a7da31587a9c618
* **Where did you get dlib**: Official github repository
* **Platform**: any
* **Compiler**: any

issue_title: doctests are broken on Travis (OS X)
issue_body:
This is because `HOMEBREW_NO_AUTO_UPDATE=1` (https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/commit/0a22bccdd624f2ad981f18df16c2b52908ddb7e8) keeps us back at an earlier version of `gcc`.

See an [pre-`0a22bcc` build][1] which has `gcc==7.2.0` and a [post-`0a22bcc` build][2] which has `gcc=6.2.0`.

According to https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-ci/issues/8552#issuecomment-338432472, the best fix will be to just wait until November 15 when new OS X images are released for Travis.

[1]: https://travis-ci.org/dhermes/bezier/jobs/283517036
[2]: https://travis-ci.org/dhermes/bezier/jobs/291698356

issue_title: The documentation should be clear of whether password can be bytes or only unicode
issue_body:
All the below applies to Python 3 only.
Some backends (i.e. SecretStorage) accept bytestrings as passwords, others (the file backend) do not.

But there is some code that tries to use bytestrings as passwords and [fails](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/python-keyring/+bug/1685547) with the file backend.

We should either fix the file backend (which is now in `keyrings.alt`) and make sure all other backends support bytes too, or fix the documentation to clearly say that the password should be a unicode string.

issue_title: Scapy route6 configuration
issue_body:
I'm using scapy to build some IPv6 packets. I'm having trouble in the communications between hosts on the same network and with external links as well, I'm only able to communicate with my router. issue_title: Total return calculation when shorting
issue_body:
Hi,

Thanks for a fantastic library.

When using weights of -1 (i.e. shorting) I get different results to what I would expect. For example take a simple test stock that starts at 100, goes up to 110, then falls to 95. Set the weight to -1 for all dates (we are shorting):
```
names = ['test']
dates = pd.date_range(start='2017-01-01',end='2017-01-03')

df = pd.DataFrame(
    [100.0,110.0,95.0],
    index = dates,
    columns = names
)

w = df.copy()
w[:] = -1

test = bt.Strategy('test', [WeighTarget(w),
                                    bt.algos.Rebalance()])

t = bt.Backtest(test, df)
res = bt.run(t)
res.display()

Stat                 test
-------------------  ----------
Start                2016-12-31
End                  2017-01-03
Risk-free rate       0.00%
Total Return         2.27%
....
```

My manual calculation for the total return is 5.26% (you effectively sold at 100 and purchased at 95).

(Perhaps the difference has to do with how much capital is considered allocated. However I have tried a few different assumptions for this but still can't match the reported 2.27%)

Please let me know if you need any more info.

Kind regards
Daniel

issue_title: distinguish relative vs absolute transform
issue_body:
gdspy doesn't seem to distinguish between relative vs absolute transform for AREF/SREF records. For example, `CellReference.to_gds()` contains the following lines:

```python
    if self.x_reflection:
        word += 0x8000
    if not (self.magnification is None):
        word += 0x0004
        values += struct.pack('>2h', 12, 0x1B05) \
            + _eight_byte_real(self.magnification)
    if not (self.rotation is None):
        word += 0x0002
        values += struct.pack('>2h', 12, 0x1C05) \
            + _eight_byte_real(self.rotation)
```
0x0004 (absolute magnification) and 0x0002 (absolute rotation) flags in STRANS are always set when there's magnification or rotation. 

Unfortunately, absolute mag/rot should be avoided in hierarchical designs with many nested levels, where absolute transforms quickly get out of control. In most cases, we should only use relative magnify/rotate in designs. This is done by clearing these two flags, while still appending the MAG and/or ANGLE records.

In short, gdspy should support and distinguish between absolute and relative transforms, with relative transform being the default.

The following page documents this matter with great details:
http://boolean.klaasholwerda.nl/interface/bnf/gdsformat.html#rec_strans

issue_title: X509Certificate filled not according to spec
issue_body:
Currently the ds:X509Certificate element gets filled from OpenSSL.crypto.dump_certificate(), but the problem here is that it also adds headers and footers like `-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----`. This is not allowed by the XMLDSig specification. Reading e.g. http://www.w3.org/TR/2002/REC-xmldsig-core-20020212/#sec-X509Data or http://www.w3.org/TR/xmldsig-core2/#sec-X509Data you note that:

* The X509Certificate element, which contains a base64-encoded [X509v3] certificate
* The schema has: `<element name="X509Certificate" type="base64Binary"/>`
* All the examples start immediately with MII...
* base64Binary disallows the `-`

Relevant line is 399 in `__init__.py`. Looking at how to resolve this.

issue_title: Let PandasPDB's and PandasMOL2's distance method accept an additional dataframe for comparison
issue_body:
The current signature of PandasMOL2's `distance` methods is 

    distance(self, xyz=(0.00, 0.00, 0.00))

where the pair-wise distance of all atoms in `PandasMOL2.df` are compared to the `xyz` distance. This can be quite wasteful in certain instances; for example, if we are only interested in the distance to certain atoms. 

The signature should be changed to 

    distance(self, df=None, xyz=(0.00, 0.00, 0.00))

where the behavior remains unchanged if `df=None`, but if and argument (DataFrame) for `df` is provided, it will be used instead of `PandasMOL2.df`

issue_title: `intersect` with multiple `-b` arguments
issue_body:
In recent BEDTools versions, `intersect` can accept a list of filenames as the `-b` argument. `pybedtools` has long supported flexible input types for `b`, for example:
- filename
- pybedtools.BedTool object
- list of (chrom, start, stop) tuples
- list of pybedtools.Interval objects
- list of strings (passed directly to stdin)

It's this last one that causes a problem. There's currently no heuristic to determine whether a list of strings should be considered filenames or intervals.

I think this can be solved by checking to see if the first item in the list can be converted to an Interval object. Consuming an input iterator would be avoided if this check is only done if the input is a list. That means filenames can't be provided as iterators, but hopefully that shouldn't be a problem.

issue_title: PEP 484 typing library
issue_body:
Since we got (limited) pep-0484 annotation support now in linter, let's take it a step further and look at the `typing` module understand. `typing` contains many parts, and looking at it prompted more questions than answers, so here an attempt to discuss them, and possibly get to a small plan to move forward

### scope

Intention here is to only look at the use of the `typing` module in already working type hints. For now this would be the function parameter and return value hints. Probably we'll get for free that it will also work in docstring hints, but not sure anyone will ever do that. So out of scope for this discussion is other PEP-0484 ways of annotating (through comments or stub files).

### typing module
The `typing` module is part of python 3.5+, and can be used on older versions through the pypi version (`typing`). So far only looked at the "simple" classes (e.g. `Union`), but there doesn't seem to be any magic there; with which I mean, something jedi can use to automagically infer what it should do. Perhaps I'm mistaken here, in that case, please put me straight.

### steps
First we have to figure out when we're actually dealing with the `typing` module. I recon that in python 3.5 it's easier to see if `import typing` is actually importing the standard typing module (or some custom module also named typing). In python <3.5, it will always be a custom module, and I think that jedi can/should only interpret type-hints using the `typing` module, if the module is imported toplevel, with the name `typing` (I may mess up some python-terms here, but I think it's clear what I mean). It also means that any such module will be interpreted that way. This means that as long as `typing` is imported in one of the ways below, we will recognise it, and assume it to be the (python 3.5-backported) `typing` module:

```
import typing
def test(x: typing.Any): ...
```

```
from typing import Any
def test(x: Any): ...
```

```
import typing as t
def test(x: t.Any):...
```

It means that not supported situations are where one installs the `typing` module under some other name or as part of a package (in these cases jedi will not realise that it should interpret the module as the `typing` module). It also means that if your do the following, you will probably get wrong behaviour:

```
[main.py]
import typing
def test(x: typing.Any): ...

[typing.py]
Any=str
```

I think the last case can be supported by jedi if we would really really want to, but I don't think it will be a major problem to have this specific case fail.

### Items within typing module
My current estimation of no "force" that will jedi-magically make the `typing` module work, probably means manually implementing each functionality manually. In the following list I try to determine what priority things should have. Probably this list is far from complete; I guess everything I leave off means no priority for now. Priorities are based in how useful I would expect them to be in the codebase of my current daytime project --- a very subjective measurement indeed, so feedback is appreciated. Note that priority does not imply order: low priority but easy to implement things may be picked up before high-priority ones.

- [`Any`][1]: low priority, not very useful for completions, however a case can be made that a function `def test(x: typing.Any): ...` should not have the type of `x` inferred from a later call to `test(1)`.
- [`Union`][2]: high priority
-`Optional`: `Optional[X] == Union[X, None]` high priority; even through for type-hinting, using `Optional[X]` is not very useful, it must be understood by jedi (probably to be just `X`)
- [`Tuple`][3]: mid priority. Is there support for tuple-types in jedi already? Two variants: `Tuple[X, Y, etc]` and `Tuple[X, ...]` meaning undefined length of type `X`.
- [`Callable`][4]: low priority; perhaps not a very good reason, but I use very few callbacks. Will have to 
- [`Typevar`][5]: high priority; It seems to me that supporting these will be very useful for correctly inferring return-types for library functions. In addition, `Typevar("A", str, int)` gives type information for any parameters of that type. I do think that correctly implementing this could be hard without diving into the inner bowels of jedi...
- `SupportsInt`, `SupportsFloat`, `SupportsAbs`, `SupportsRound`, `Reversible`, `Container`: low priority, less useful for completions, since they all only define one (or 2) method(s), that should not be called directly, and not very useful in returntype from those functions.
- `Iterable`, `Iterator`: mid priority, help infer type of loops variables and `next()` calls
- `AbstractSet`, `MutableSet`: mid priority, help infer type of loops variables
- `Mapping`, `MutableMapping`, `Dict`:  high priority, help infer type of loops variables and `__getitem__` calls, however only for homogenous maps
- `Sequence`, `MutableSequence`, `List`: high priority: help infer type of loops variables and `__getitem__`
- `ByteString`: low priority
- `MappingView`, `KeysView`, `ItemsView`, `ValuesView`: mid priority, help infer type of loops variables
- `Generator`: low priority
- `io`: low priority
- `re`: low priority
- `NamedTuple`: low priority
- `cast`: low priority
- `get_type_hints`: irrelevant
- `no_type_check`, `no_type_check_decorator`: not supported for now


### plan of approach

issue_title: XEP-0084: Try to remove the Global Avatar Lock
issue_body:
The current avatar implementation uses a lock over the data structures, preventing more than one simultaneous query for avatar metadata.

This is bad, because when the s2s to a peers server fails, we may block the whole avatar subsystem for quite some time.

If possible, we should find a way to remove the Global Avatar Lock.

issue_title: GPG signatures for source validation
issue_body:
As we all know, today more than ever before, it is crucial to be able to trust our computing environments. One of the main difficulties that package maintainers of GNU/Linux distributions face, is the difficulty to verify the authenticity and the integrity of the source code. With GPG signatures it is possible for packagers to verify source code releases quickly and easily.

#### Overview of the required tasks:
* Create and/or use a **[4096-bit RSA keypair][1]** for the file signing
* Use a **[strong, unique, secret passphrase][2]** for the key
* Upload the public key to a **[key server][3]** and **[publish the full fingerprint][4]**
* **[Sign][5]** every new Git **[commit][6]** and **[tag][7]**
* Create **[signed][8], [compressed][9]** (xz --best) release **archives**
* Upload a **[strong message digest][10]** (sha512) of the archive
* Configure **[HTTPS][11]** for your download server

### GPGit
[GPGit][12] is meant to bring GPG to the masses. It is not only a Python script that automates the process of [creating new signed Git releases with GPG][13], but also a [quick-start-guide][14] for learning how to use GPG. GPGit integrates perfectly with the [Github Release API][15] for uploading.

The security status of GNU/Linux projects will be tracked in the [Linux Security Database][16]. If you have any further questions, do not hesitate to [contact me][17] personally. Thanks for your help in making GNU/Linux projects more secure by using GPG signatures.

Thanks in advance.

[1]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#12-key-generation
[2]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#11-strong-unique-secret-passphrase
[3]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#21-send-key-to-a-key-server
[4]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#22-publish-full-fingerprint
[5]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#31-configure-git-gpg-key
[6]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#32-commit-signing
[7]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#33-create-signed-git-tag
[8]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#42-sign-the-archive
[9]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#41-create-compressed-archive
[10]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#43-create-the-message-digest
[11]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#51-configure-https-download-server
[12]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit
[13]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#script-usage
[14]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#gpg-quick-start-guide
[15]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#52-upload-to-github
[16]: https://github.com/NicoHood/LSD

issue_title: Add support for CSI n K: EL – Erase in Line
issue_body:
Migrated from https://code.google.com/p/colorama/issues/detail?id=30
Reported by maruel@google.com, Oct 20, 2011

As described on Wikipedia:
"Erases part of the line. If n is zero (or missing), clear from cursor to the end of the line. If n is one, clear from cursor to beginning of the line. If n is two, clear entire line. Cursor position does not change."

What steps will reproduce the problem?

issue_title: Boolean arguments don't work.
issue_body:
So query variables that expect a boolean value are case-sensitive. They only accept `true` or `false`. However the default string representation of a bool in Python is `'True'` and `'False'`. Because of this passing a bool argument won't do anything as the API will fallback to whatever the default is.

This example will not return any results because `include_adult` will fallback to its default of `false`:
`tmdb.Search().movie(query='She\'s so small', include_adult=True)`

Passing a string does work:
`tmdb.Search().movie(query='She\'s so small', include_adult='true')`

Kind of messy to work around that with if-else statements or `str(expression).lower()`.

issue_title: RFC Confusing seek implementation
issue_body:
Hi all

I feel that the current seek implementation in the SimpleConsumer is rather confusing, there feels like there are two issues here:

1. In the case of a consumer with multiple partitions, the divide by partitions and find the offset does not seem like it would render the desired effect, which is described somewhat in #67 

2. The offset whence behaviour works from a delta, that is with say whence 0 it is not seek to this absolute offset, but rather seek to the ```first available message offset``` + ```seek offset```.

The API as described does make sense in the context of being analogous to ```fseek()``` but is confusing with regard to kafka as a queue, since at present 0.8.0 does not automatically maintain client offsets, most clients are forced to maintain these directly.

Since the ```seek``` behaviour always adds the lowest offset and the delta it becomes difficult to actually maintain these numbers, especially as there is no natural way to get the lowest offset in a partition (which would allow computing the delta ...)

I feel that the API in general is very *confusing* and **extremely** subtle, I have for my own purposes created a new api call called ```seek_absolute``` which can be found on this commit (https://github.com/GregBowyer/kafka-python/commit/056cba565f356787f1b027a881a7487be5a758ce)  but rather than make a pull request, I think that more discussion is really needed over how the seek contract should work in general

Thoughts ?

issue_title: Test with https by default
issue_body:
I think that the library must try to use https instead of http, simply because by definition it's more secure.

» it must try to connect to "https://framapiaf.org" if "http://framapiaf.org" is configured. If it works, he must use it. If request fails, it toggles on http only.

issue_title: Licensing question
issue_body:
Hi,
thanks for making pytricia available as Free Software!

Regarding the license in detail:
 * Wouldn't make sense to have a library like this be available under 
a license with less Freedom protection? (The FSF for instance recommends the https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.en.html#apache2 Apache 2.0 license if you want to do this.
Another option would be the GNU Lesser GPL v>=2.1). Usually for a tiny library: less Freedom protection can result in higher usage. This is good for Free Software because for a tiny library people will often not consider changing the license and alternatively instead of using pytricia just pick a different one.
 * Is the current license ment to be GNU GPL v==2 or GNU GPL v>=2? I suggest to add a sentence for this to the README.md. (Usually this is stated with the license file itself and GNU GPL says "or at your option later version" or so. This is good, because otherwise you'll stay incompatible with GNU GPL v>=3 or LGPL v>=3.)

To state my interest: Currently I am helping https://intelmq.org/ and they would rather replace py-radix within pyasn to get the license situation cleared.  

Best Regards

issue_title: Export error with InfluxDB 0.9
Use Glances as a NSCA client
issue_body:
As far as I understood, Glances has several running modes (standalone, server, ...) but does not propose some kind of `monitoring agent` behavior ... let me explain.

 I am currently using NSClient as a monitoring agent for my Windows hosts (more than 1000 devices). NSClient has its own checks scheduler that will run local health checks (CPU, memory, ...) on a periodical basis. Once a check run, NSClient can use NSCA protocol to send the checks results to a monitoring server like Nagios or Shinken.

 1/ I would like to replace NSClient with Glances. Each check result is sent to the monitoring server as a passive check result using NSCA (or any other protocol ...). 

 2/ I would like to use Glances as a monitoring agent that will alert my central server only when some  checks results changed. I know that I can configure Glances for running a script on warning or critical ... what about recover ? It may be interesting if this behavior would be activated on a gloabl basis. For instance, configuring a central NSCA (or HTTPS) receiver where all the checks results state change would be sent ?

 An idea to be discussed ...

issue_title: Unjsonify Function
issue_body:
Hey guys, I noticed that treelib has a to_json function, but not an unjsonify function that inputs a JSON string and builds the original tree as the output. I decided to go ahead and write an unjsonify function, which I thought might be useful:

```python
import json                                                                                                              
from treelib import Node, Tree
from collections import Hashable

# takes in a jsonified tree and returns the correct tree structure
def unjsonify(jsonTree):
    def giveKey(d):
        return d.keys()[0]
    def unjsonifyHelper(node, subtree):
        for childStruct in subtree[node]['children']: # childStruct is immutable element if base case, else dict
            if type(childStruct) == list: childStruct = tuple(childStruct) # tuples in original tree
            if isinstance(childStruct, Hashable): # base case
                newTree.create_node(childStruct, childStruct, parent=node)
            else:
                childNode = giveKey(childStruct)
                newTree.create_node(childNode, childNode, parent=node)
                unjsonifyHelper(childNode, childStruct)
    jsonTree = json.loads(jsonTree)
    root = giveKey(jsonTree)
    newTree = Tree()
    newTree.create_node(root, root)
    unjsonifyHelper(root, jsonTree)
    return newTree

# simple test below
tree = Tree()
tree.create_node('home', 'home')
tree.create_node('phone', 'phone', parent='home')
tree.create_node('laptop', 'laptop', parent='home')
tree.create_node('screen', 'screen', parent='laptop')
tree.create_node(19, 19, parent='home')
tree.create_node((1,2), (1,2), parent='screen')
j = tree.to_json()
unjsonify(j).show()   
```

There's some stuff to note here about the code:

2) The to_json function as it's written only preserves keys and not values in the tree. Would you guys be interested in a custom to_json function that saves both keys and values (which json.dumps currently doesn't do right now)?
1) I'm not 100% sure why, but json.dumps converts tuples into lists. Because treelib doesn't allow mutable elements to be keys, I've decided to turn lists back into tuples before I determine if an element is hashable or not (and would be a leaf if it's hashable), since there couldn't have been any lists as keys in the original tree anyway.

Please let me know what you guys think!

issue_title: The way we're building the nng library is an abuse of setuptools
issue_body:
Right now we're subclassing the [`build_py` class](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/build_py.py) in order to make sure that the nng library would be built by the time we needed to build an extension, but this was – apparently – a misunderstanding of how setuptools works.  I think it is also causing us to build nng _twice_ on all the CI runs, which is unfortunate.

I'm not planning on fixing this immediately, since the current situation is workable and there are more important things to do.

issue_title: speeding up kdj
issue_body:
Hi,

kdj is terribly slow, probably because the result is created step by step with list.append().
I did some speed tuning which gives about 60 times speed up, code see below.
Works only with python 3 because I used `nonlocal` in the inline function.

```python
    @classmethod
    def _get_kdjk(cls, df, n_days):
        """ Get the K of KDJ
        Overwrite stockstats.StockDataFrame methods to speed up.

        K ＝ 2/3 × (prev. K) +1/3 × (curr. RSV)
        2/3 and 1/3 are the smooth parameters.
        :param df: data
        :param n_days: calculation range
        :return: None
        """
        rsv_column = 'rsv_{}'.format(n_days)
        k_column = 'kdjk_{}'.format(n_days)

        KDJ_PARAM0, KDJ_PARAM1 = cls.KDJ_PARAM
        prev_k = 50.0

        def __add(t):
            nonlocal prev_k
            prev_k = KDJ_PARAM0 * prev_k + t
            return prev_k

        df[k_column] = [__add(t) for t in KDJ_PARAM1 * df.get(rsv_column).as_matrix()]

    @classmethod
    def _get_kdjd(cls, df, n_days):
        """ Get the D of KDJ

        D = 2/3 × (prev. D) +1/3 × (curr. K)
        2/3 and 1/3 are the smooth parameters.
        :param df: data
        :param n_days: calculation range
        :return: None
        """
        k_column = 'kdjk_{}'.format(n_days)
        d_column = 'kdjd_{}'.format(n_days)

        KDJ_PARAM0, KDJ_PARAM1 = cls.KDJ_PARAM
        prev_d = 50.0

        def __add(t):
            nonlocal prev_d
            prev_d = KDJ_PARAM0 * prev_d + t
            return prev_d

        df[d_column] = [__add(t) for t in KDJ_PARAM1 * df.get(k_column).as_matrix()]

```

issue_title: Support chromecast audio groups
issue_body:
[`get_chromecasts` in controllers.py](https://github.com/skorokithakis/catt/blob/master/catt/controllers.py#L32) ensures that all devices can be dialed by creating a new `pychromecast.Chromecast` using the device's `host` parameter. Unfortunately, for audio groups, the `host` param is mostly meaningless and chosen arbitrarily from the devices that participate in the group. This is easily visible from the output of `catt scan`:
```
> catt scan
Scanning Chromecasts...
192.168.1.75 - Bedroom - Google Inc. Google Home
192.168.1.77 - Bedroom TV - Google Inc. Eureka Dongle
192.168.1.70 - Kitchen - Google Inc. Google Home Mini
192.168.1.76 - Living Room - Google Inc. Chromecast Audio
192.168.1.70 - Kitchen - Google Inc. Google Home Mini
```
In this output it's the Kitchen device that's present twice, but this varies across runs as different elements of the group are selected.

Since all device lookups go through `get_chromecasts`, this means that any command directed at an audio group instead operates on a single arbitrary member of the group.

It _appears_ that removing the list comprehension in the final statement of `get_chromecasts` (that is, just return `devices` instead of rebuilding each one with an address) fixes this and lets catt play to chromecast audio groups, but since I don't know why that functionality was added in the first place and was too lazy to get a dev env properly set up, I'll submit a feature request instead of a PR.

issue_title: Need a robust connection & connection pool.
issue_body:
happybase currently use Thrift transport's `isOpen()` method (which is in fact the `TSocket` `isOpen()` method)  to determine [whether it should open a new connection or keep the current one](https://github.com/wbolster/happybase/blob/9cbd718c10a3089f234f1eac1236b631e1f8e7cd/happybase/connection.py#L164-L173). 

However, `isOpen()` only tells us whether you have closed this socket explicitly. (See [here](https://github.com/apache/thrift/blob/1fa273be5089070532908f159a0ccc38d7f7150c/lib/py/src/transport/TSocket.py#L44-L47) and [here](https://github.com/apache/thrift/blob/1fa273be5089070532908f159a0ccc38d7f7150c/lib/py/src/transport/TSocket.py#L72-L73) in thrift).

So, we should not use `isOpen` to determine whether we need a new connection. but to test the real socket object to see if the it is "dead" and should be closed.

Relative PR in thrift: https://github.com/apache/thrift/pull/945

issue_title: have a sensible set of default values
issue_body:
make our default gridsearch only run over a select subset of all the configurable values. 

for example, by default, take the log of our y values, scale all of our x variables to the 90th and 10th percentile at 0 and 1, truncate all outliers at the 90th percentile value, do not run clustering on our input data, etc. 

we can even let the user pass in a value from 1 - 10 for how much computing they want to do. 

.1. does no grid search at all, just runs through our default values for a linear/logistic regression, and probably xgboost

.3. tries to optimize the models now

.5. tries some feature engineering optimization (if we scale or not, if we natural log or not, etc.)

.7. tries more models

.9. runs clustering on input. 

.10. everything imaginable. probably not realistic for any dataset of more than a few hundred thousand rows. 

let the user pass this into train. everything else goes into customized_train (where you can still pick a compute number, and just override our defaults with whatever other params you pass in).

issue_title: plumbum.cli.Flag shouldn't print the default on execution of --help
issue_body:
```
./a.py --help
--no-ut                     Set this flag to skip the unit-tests; *the default is True*
./b.py --help
--release                      Deploy in release mode (no QA baggage); *the default is True*
```

This is *very* confusing...
It's pointless to print the default value for flags which are just switching a state, and the default value is for the in-code variable, which for the above are:

```python
    ut = cli.Flag("--no-ut", default=True, help="Set this flag to skip the unit-tests")
    dev = cli.Flag("--release", default=True, help="Deploy in release mode (no QA baggage)")
```

a fix can be:
```python
--- a/plumbum/cli/switches.py
+++ b/plumbum/cli/switches.py
@@ -179,7 +179,7 @@ class SwitchAttr(object):
 
     def __init__(self, names, argtype = str, default = None, list = False, argname = "VALUE", **kwargs):
         self.__doc__ = "Sets an attribute"  # to prevent the help message from showing SwitchAttr's docstring
-        if "help" in kwargs and default:
+        if "help" in kwargs and default and argtype is not None:
             kwargs["help"] += "; the default is %r" % (default,)
 
         switch(names, argtype = argtype, argname = argname, list = list, **kwargs)(self)
```

issue_title: @ajax decorator does not set http error code in JSONResponse
issue_body:
When raising an exception in a function decorated with @ajax, the http status code is not set according to the error code:

HTTP status is 200 (OK):
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/839540/7503676/d2d3f9ea-f444-11e4-8326-44a2b9963163.png)

However, the JSON-content is set correctly:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/839540/7503727/1bfda7e2-f445-11e4-9f4b-3936ef7c2966.png)

This can be fixed by changing response.py as follows (added status=data['status']):

```python
class JSONResponse(HttpResponse):
    """
    Return a JSON serialized HTTP response
    """

    def __init__(self, data, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        This returns a object that we send as json content using
        utils.serialize_to_json, that is a wrapper to json.dumps
        method using a custom class to handle models and querysets. Put your
        options to serialize_to_json in kwargs, other options are used by
        response.
        """
        if not 'sort_keys' in kwargs:
            kwargs['sort_keys'] = settings.DEBUG

        super(JSONResponse, self).__init__(
            status=data['status'],
            content=serialize_to_json(data, *args, **kwargs),
            content_type='application/json'
        )
```

Result:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/839540/7503782/709cfd48-f445-11e4-8e72-6e8a1a1f8c3c.png)

issue_title: Better stateful testing
issue_body:
Hypothesis’s stateful testing is extremely powerful and if you’re not using it you should be.

But… I’ll be the first to admit it’s a little hard to use. The generic API is fine. It’s quite low level but it’s easy to use. The rule based stuff should be easy to use, but there’s a bit too much boiler plate and bundles of variables are a weird second class citizen.

What I’d like to be able to do is make them just behave like strategies, where the strategy’s evaluation is deferred until execution time, so you can use them as if they were any other strategy and everything should Just Work.

I would also like the syntax for using it to be more unified with the normal given syntax. Ideally it would be nice if every given invocation had an implicit state machine associated with it so as to unify the two approaches.

issue_title: Django fixture support does not work with Django 1.7
issue_body:
Try calling `fixture(models(Foo))` with a model and you'll get a stacktrace like this:

```
  File "x/tests.py", line 17, in <module>
    SITE = fixture(models(Site))
  File "x/hypothesis/hypothesis/extra/django/fixtures.py", line 104, in fixture
    return Fixture(strategy, constraint, execute)
  File "x/hypothesis/hypothesis/extra/django/fixtures.py", line 51, in __init__
    self._set_template()
  File "x/hypothesis/hypothesis/extra/django/fixtures.py", line 96, in _set_template
    old_name, verbosity, False)
TypeError: destroy_test_db() takes at most 3 arguments (4 given)
```

The problem is that the third argument of `destroy_test_db` (`keepdb=False`) was only [introduced in Django 1.8](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/testing/advanced/#django.db.connection.creation.destroy_test_db).

issue_title: EventReader's may associate packets with an event that don't belong to it.
issue_body:
The way unified2 logging works, the packets (or any non-event record) following an event record may not be associated with that event, but an event that happened before it.

Currently the record aggregator will bundle all non-event records with the most recent event until a new event record is received.  Instead it will need to verify that the record does belong to the event that is currently being aggregated.

This also means that an Event object may not contain all the packet records associated it with it, as there may be an event record in the unified2 log file before a packet record for the previous event is seen.

I think the only solution is that the EventReader's make a best effort to gather all the records together in an event.  Perhaps the aggregator could have a timeout where it holds onto multiple events for an amount of time waiting for more records, but there could still be a record that appears outside that window.

Still needs some thought.

issue_title: Resolve .has_value, .has_default, .has_values and value proxy inconsistency
issue_body:
What does having a value mean?

a. it means to have a custom value
b. it means that either a default or a custom value is set, so calling `.value` won't raise an exception

Who does what?
 - `ConfigItem.has_value` (a)
 - `ConfigManager.has_values` (a)
 - `ConfigSectionProxy.has_values` (a)
 - `ConfigValueProxy` returns items which meet (b)

Solution:
 - Rename `.has_value` to `.has_custom_value` (or something else) and make `.has_value` behave as in (b)

issue_title: FEN generation doesn't handle en passant
issue_body:
After 1. e4 e5, the FEN generated is 
```
rnbqkbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/4P3/8/PPPP1PPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 0 2
```
ignoring the en passant square. According to the [FEN definition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forsyth%E2%80%93Edwards_Notation), the correct FEN should be 
```
rnbqkbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/4P3/8/PPPP1PPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq e6 0 2
```

The en passant square is supposed to be included whether or not a pawn in is position to capture en passant. Should be as simple as testing for a two-square pawn move and then setting the en passant square behind it.

issue_title: WeekdayPositionExpression: unexpected keyword argument 'option' and 'weekday'
issue_body:
The Python (2.5) report following error:
  File "C:\programy.win\Python25\lib\site-packages\apscheduler\triggers.py", lin
e 35, in compile_single
    
    #!python
        return compiler(**match.groupdict())
    TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'option'
    

Solution:
Original code with error:

    
    #!python
    
    class WeekdayPositionExpression(object):
        options = ['1st', '2nd', '3rd', '4th', '5th', 'last']
        value_re = re.compile(r'(?P<option>%s) +(?P<weekday>(?:\d+|\w+))'
                              % '|'.join(options), re.IGNORECASE)
    

Fixed line:

    
    #!python
        value_re = re.compile(r'(?P<**option_name**>%s) +(?P<weekday_name>(?:\d+|\w+))'
                              % '|'.join(options), re.IGNORECASE)
    

----------------------------------------

issue_title: Separate dispatch handler function calling from filtering logic
issue_body:
Right now the existing FileSystemEventHandlers' ``dispatch`` functions contain both the logic for filtering events and the logic for calling the right handler functions. This makes it difficult to patch in support for things like asyncio or gevent, which could reuse the filtering and dispatch logic but need to call the handler functions in a different way. 

This could be solved by splitting the creation of ``_method_map`` out of the ``dispatch`` function, so that just that could be overridden in ``FileSystemEventHandler`` subclasses.

issue_title: Logic adapter for some kind of facet search
issue_body:
I'm considering a problem where there may be a product database or some other searchable data set.

I'd like to be able to extract keywords and perform searches, and return responses that may interest the user.

It looks like I'd have to write a logic adapter, which I should be able to do as a developer.  But I am unsure how to incorporate this into a training set, or whether a contextually aware sequence is possible.

E.g say I have cars, houses and trucks and a conversation goes like this:

"Hey there, I'd like to build a house, I'd like to know more about the Alto floorplans?"
<searches database, finds House category, title Alto, 3 floorplans.  Creates a new context.>
"No problems, I can see 3 floorplans for Alto: 'Modern', 'Classic' and 'Entertainer'.  How can I help with these?"
"Which is the cheapest?"
<Logic adapter detects an existing context, so further searches retain previous information.  Price filter applied>
"The Classic is our base range.  Would you like to speak to a consultant about the classic?"
"Yes please" <a "no" here would reset the context and release the logic adapter.>
"Please enter your email address and / or phone number and we'll be in touch shortly!"

Is something like this possible, e.g. real time context-sensitive searches?

Apologies if this is the wrong forum for this question, happy to be pointed in the right direction.  I'm just getting started with this stuff...

Thanks!

issue_title: Should setting __slots__ be the default
issue_body:
In point 5 of issue #9, @ilevkivskyi suggests that setting __slots__ should be the default, and setting slots=False would allow the caller to opt out of this behavior.

Given the typical use cases for this feature, I tend to agree. I'm opening this issue for discussion of this point.

issue_title: Supporting immutable instances
issue_body:
attrs has a frozen=True decorator parameter that causes all instances to be read-only. It does this by adding a __setattr__ to the class which disallows overwriting attributes.

I like this feature, and I assume we should do the same thing. If anyone disagrees, please discuss here.

issue_title: Consider exposing _MISSING in the public API
issue_body:
I wrote [an integration for `dataclasses` in a pretty printer library](https://github.com/tommikaikkonen/prettyprinter/blob/b2a1e30884316349e54f094eedd4cc5139b4838a/prettyprinter/extras/dataclasses.py). In order for the integration to work well, I need the `_MISSING` constant value to determine if a field default or default factory is set.

`attrs` uses a public constant `attr.NOTHING` to facilitate that, whereas PEP557 explicitly says that `_MISSING` is private:

>The `_MISSING` value is a sentinel object used to detect if the default and default_factory parameters are provided. Users should never use `_MISSING` or depend on its value. This sentinel is used because `None` is a valid value for default.

For code that inspects dataclasses, it is highly useful to be able to determine if a field default is set or not. The current public API doesn't provide a way to do that.

[Discussion on whether `attrs.NOTHING` should be public or private](https://github.com/python-attrs/attrs/issues/254)

issue_title: Extra API calls when getting attributes of Cell instance
issue_body:
In 1.1.3 method `get_all_values(returnas='cell')` does retrieve all cell's attributes in single API call. But this isn't useful because when you access attribute of such cell, internal code does fetch as separate API call for every cell. This happens even when Cell instance has all the attributes from Spreadsheet.

Example:
```python
cells = sheet.get_all_values(returnas='cell')  # single API call to get all cells with all its attributes - cool
print(cells[0][0].color)  # color property calls fetch which leads to Spreadsheets API call - not cool
print(cells[0][1].color)  # another API call - not cool
# etc
print(cells[0][2]._color)  # no API call, _color has value from Spreadsheet
```

issue_title: Executor to cancel/fail futures which do not complete by a deadline
issue_body:
I would like to be able to create futures which are failed automatically if they don't complete within a certain amount of time.

Compared to TimeoutExecutor, the difference here is that the deadline will be applied directly on the futures, no matter how the result is queried.  One of the major benefits of that is that concurrent.futures methods like as_completed would also be subject to the deadline.

Once the deadline is reached the behavior should be something like:

- attempt to cancel the delegate future
- set this future's result to failed
-- Maybe with a new exception class, so it won't be mixed up with TimeoutError?

issue_title: EnumField default_error_messages looks wrong
Allow panflute filters to be autodownloaded from github.
issue_body:
Note: I think this is outside the scope of panflute, and there should be a separate repo that deals with this (we can add it to panflute's dependencies)

## Implementation:

- The package should fetch a `filters.yaml` file from the repo of an organization repo. This file should have a list of items, each with two fields (name and url).
- A filter consists of a python file (for now let's limit ourselves to one file) and optionally a yaml file (e.g. headers.py and headers.csv)
- The YAML file contains metadata about the filter. This includes: name, description, author, date, version (!), etc.
- If at some point way in the future we want to extend this, we can do it easily through the yaml (eg: allow for more than one python file, more than just python files, versioning, example input and output markdown files).
- The url should point to either the `.py` file or the `.yaml` file
- Both files shoud be copied to datadir/filters (or to whatever path the user asks)

Finally, this utility should allow for a few suboptions:

- `??? install somefilter`
- `??? update somefilter`
- `??? install filter1 filter2 filter3`
- `??? uninstall filter1`


Also see:
- Bower: https://bower.herokuapp.com/packages
- Conda forge: https://github.com/conda-forge/feedstocks
- ST Package control: https://github.com/wbond/package_control_channel/

issue_title: Replace '\n' w/ os.linesep
Preserve order while dumping/loading xml array
issue_body:
Load the below file using anyconfig:

```
<configuration scan="false" scanPeriod="5 seconds">
  <appender class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender" name="STDOUT">
    <encoder class="classic.encoder.PatternLayoutEncoder">
      <Pattern>%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS}  [%thread] %-5level %logger{36} - %method - %msg%n</Pattern>
    </encoder>
  </appender>
  <logger additivity="false" level="INFO" name="com.site">
    <appender-ref ref="STDOUT"/>
  </logger>
  <property name="LOG_HOME" value="/opt/logs"/>
  <root level="ERROR">
    <appender-ref ref="STDOUT"/>
  </root>
</configuration>
```

and dump it using anyconfig, it dumps as shown below:

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<configuration scan="false" scanPeriod="5 seconds">
	<property name="LOG_HOME" value="/opt/logs" />
	<appender name="STDOUT" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
		<encoder class="classic.encoder.PatternLayoutEncoder">
			<Pattern>
				%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS}  [%thread] %-5level %logger{36} - %method - %msg%n
			</Pattern>
		</encoder>
 	</appender>
	<root level="ERROR">
		<!-- <appender-ref ref="FILE" /> -->
		<appender-ref ref="STDOUT" />
  	</root>
	<logger name="com.site" level="INFO"  additivity="false">
		<!-- <appender-ref ref="FILE" /> -->
		<appender-ref ref="STDOUT" />
	</logger>
</configuration>
```

As seen here the order is not preserved (property comes first, ordering of root level is also screwed up). There are [cases](https://logback.qos.ch/codes.html#appender_order) where ordering in xml matters. So can anyconfig load/dump xml in the same order on disk.

issue_title: STS credentials reused improperly
issue_body:
## Bug Report
STS credentials for one AWS account seem to be reused when connecting to another AWS account before they expire.

I want to write a function to check limits/thresholds in multiple AWS accounts at once.  The code below returns number of S3 buckets in  ACCOUNT01 account correctly but it is incorrect for  ACCOUNT02 account as the STS credentials from ACCOUNT01 account are reused.

Testing code to reproduce the issue:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python
from awslimitchecker.checker import AwsLimitChecker

def foo(event):
    # input parameters for checker
    service = event['service']
    region = event['region']
    account_name = event['account_name']
    account_id = str(event['accounts'][account_name]['id'])
    account_role = event['account_role']

    checker = AwsLimitChecker(account_id=account_id, 
                              account_role=account_role, region=region)
    checker.check_thresholds(service)
    result = checker.get_limits(service)[service]

    for limit_name, limit in result.items():
        limitstr = str(limit.get_limit())
        usagestr = str(limit.get_current_usage_str())

        print("Service usage: account_name: {}, region: {}, service: {}, \
              limit_name: {}, limit: {}, limit_usage: {}".format(account_name, \
              region, service, limit_name, limitstr, usagestr))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    event = {
        u'service': 'S3',
        u'region': 'us-east-1',
        u'account_name': 'ACCOUNT01',
        u'account_role': 'ALC_ROLE',
        u'accounts': {
            u'ACCOUNT01': {
                u'id': XXXXXXXXXXX01
            },
            u'ACCOUNT02': {
                u'id': XXXXXXXXXXX02
            }

        }
    }

    foo(event)

    event['account_name'] = 'ACCOUNT02'

    foo(event)
```

### Testing Assistance
So far, I haven't been  able to update ```Test_Connectable``` test so ```tox```  wouldn't return any failures.


### Version
Reproduced on versions  0.5.1 and 0.5.0.

### Installation Method
Installed with pip.

issue_title: Allow variables to be prefixed with "export"
issue_body:
Hi there. In Rust there's also a [dotenv library](https://crates.io/crates/dotenv#examples) which is very similar but has a nice additional feature:

> You can optionally prefix each line with the word export, which will conveniently allow you to source the whole file on your shell.

So a `.env` file essentially has extended syntax which makes the following valid:

```sh
export MY_API_KEY="blah"
```

This comes in handy because during local development because some command line developer tools need access to the same environment variables that your application does (e.g. if you want to use `curl` to test an API endpoint that your application uses).

issue_title: `from eralchemy import render_er` is broken from #16
issue_body: This is my own darn fault. I'm about to submit a PR that fixes it while retaining the advantages gained in #16. Basically, it makes more sense to leave `render_er` in `__init__.py` and move `version` to `version.py`. The only thing that has to change is the version string import in `setup.py`. This also seems much less likely to break downstream packages.

issue_title: This code is causing failures for JSON responses
issue_body:
https://github.com/striglia/pyramid_swagger/blob/e89cf43fc91d382d9255669e0cd9f12543988c8b/pyramid_swagger/tween.py#L545-L549

Caused this ticket:

https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid/pull/2971

In WebOb 1.7 it was changed so that those content-types that don't have a charset parameter (mostly application/*) would no longer send an charset content-type parameter.

JSON is specified as always being either UTF-8 or UTF-16/32 with BOM. There is no charset parameter, and clients are explicitly told to ignore it. To be a good internet citizen you should be strict in what you send... and WebOb 1.7 really tried to make that a reality.

WebOb 1.7 introduced `Response.default_body_encoding` so that `Response.text` may be used even in cases where there is no charset defined for the content-type, although I wouldn't necessarily encourage this use, instead I would recommend setting response.body with a binary type string instead after properly encoding the data.

Thanks!

issue_title: App not started by the time handlers are called?
issue_body:
I'm trying to sort out an error I'm seeing when use this package. In one of my handlers I access `request.app.loop`, but sanic is reporting that I can only access the loop after the app is started. Here's the error:

```
  File "/Users/abingham/.virtualenvs/cyber-dojo/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic/app.py", line 556, in handle_request
    response = await response
  File "/Users/abingham/.virtualenvs/cyber-dojo/lib/python3.6/site-packages/repl_container_python-1.0.0-py3.6.egg/repl_container_python/handlers.py", line 64, in create_repl_handler
    loop=request.app.loop,
  File "/Users/abingham/.virtualenvs/cyber-dojo/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic/app.py", line 73, in loop
    'Loop can only be retrieved after the app has started '
sanic.exceptions.SanicException: Loop can only be retrieved after the app has started running. Not supported with `create_server` function
```

I'm using a test fixture following the description in the pytest-sanic docs, and my configuration does in fact work for other handlers (i.e. those that don't access the loop). My fixture setup looks like this:

```python
import pytest
from sanic.websocket import WebSocketProtocol

import repl_container_python.app

@pytest.yield_fixture
def app():
    app = repl_container_python.app.create_app()
    yield app

@pytest.fixture(scope='function')
def test_cli(loop, app, test_client):
    client = test_client(app, protocol=WebSocketProtocol)
    return loop.run_until_complete(client)
```

And my test looks like this:
```python
class TestReplCreation:
    async def test_repl_creation_responds_with_201(self, test_cli):
        resp = await test_cli.post('/', data=b'[]')
        assert resp.status == 201
```

I'm seeing the failure in the handling of the `post()` call.

Any idea what's going on? I've look at the pytest-sanic implementation a bit, and it looks like maybe there's a missing call to `TestServer.start_server()`, but that's just educated guessing. I've followed the docs closely, so I feel like I'm missing something or just being dense.

hi,

I've read several conference papers relating to `pythran` but still need to ask few questions. It's great if `pythran` developers could discuss.

Before knowing `pythran`, I only really paid attention to `cython` and `numba`. I chose `cython` over `numba` because cython is very portable. And cython works really well with C++ class.

Later I know `pythran` when watching your talk (youtube). From reading your papers, it seems that `pythran` dev really want to focus on `openmp` (and simd too?) with more control on how openmp work (similiar to c/c++); which is very great bonus.

so,

* How `pythran` can survive among many other compilers? 
* how `pythran` can support calling c++ class like `cython` (great thing in cython)
* it's likely that `pythran` heavily depend on `boost` and `nt2`, which require `conda` to manage the installation. How `pythran` can "beat" `numba`? :D 
* is there any intention to speeding up compiling code? Linking cythonzied code to c++ lib is quite slow but compiling `.so` file in `pythran` is even slower.

(still have other questions but don't remember)

Hai

issue_title: wrong (numpy) calculations
issue_body:
I start a new thread, since the ndarray.bool solved the previous problem and the code compiles!

I will repost the code: 

#pythran export wave(int)
import numpy as np

def wave(n):

    Nx = n
    number_of_threads = 4
    t0 = 0
    x0 = 0
    alpha = np.sqrt(2.)/np.pi
    dx = 12./Nx
    dt = 0.5*dx/alpha
    Nt = int(5.*np.pi/dt)
    c = alpha*dt/dx
    u = np.zeros((Nx,Nt))
    x = np.zeros(Nx)
    t = np.zeros(Nt)

    #omp parallel for num_threads(number_of_threads) private(i) shared(Nx,x0,x,dx)
    for i in range(Nx):
        x[i] = x0+i*dx

    #omp parallel for num_threads(number_of_threads) private(i) shared(Nt,t0,dt,t)
    for i in range(Nt):
        t[i] = t0 + i*dt
    
    for i in range(Nx):
        if x[i]>=2 and x[i]<=4:
            u[i,0] = np.sin(np.pi*x[i])
        else:
            u[i,0] = 0
        #omp parallel for shared(Nt,u) private(j)
        for j in range(Nt):
            u[0,j]=0
            u[Nx-1,j]=0

    for j in range(Nt-1):
        #omp parallel for num_threads(number_of_threads) private(i) shared(u,Nx,j,c)
        for i in range(Nx-1):
            u[i,j+1]=u[i,j]-(c/2.)*(u[i+1,j]-u[i-1,j])+(np.power(c,2.)/2.)*(u[i+1,j]-2.*u[i,j]+u[i-1,j])
    print(u[:,0])
    return u


u[:,0] is actually the initial wave that will be computed in time. So the correct form would be 
sin(πx) but I get something weird computed by the pythran module as you can see in the following image:

https://drive.google.com/open?id=1O9r_iqtTKaRwiawrGfRkEwJkicc4GJI8
I printed the u[:,0] from inside the module, or the calculation itself and it gives strange incorrect numbers.

issue_title: Add tests
Allow waitFor* actions when rendering.
issue_body:
When rendering a page, you sometimes need to wait for AJAX requests to complete. While using a sleep time can help, that method will easily fail if the network is slow or congested. Either expose the Pyppeteer page object or allow the caller to specify some `waitFor*` configuration. 

For example,  [`waitForNavigation()`](https://miyakogi.github.io/pyppeteer/reference.html#pyppeteer.page.Page.waitForNavigation) lets you specify a specific event to wait for, like [`networkidle0`](https://github.com/GoogleChrome/puppeteer/blob/master/docs/api.md#pagewaitfornavigationoptions), which usually indicates all network-dependent JS code is done.

issue_title: yf.download start & end dates error
issue_body:
The download function just works when the start & end date parameters are static strings, the problem comes when you try to format a date value to string, the download function does not recognize the parameters and throws and error.

```
import fix_yahoo_finance as yf
import datetime as dt

actual_date = dt.date.today()
last_month_date = actual_date - dt.timedelta(days=30)

data = yf.download("SPY", start=actual_date.isoformat(), end=last_month_date.isoformat())
df = pd.DataFrame(data=data)
```

The solution to this is creating static strings, f.e.:
```
actual_date = "2017-09-22"
last_month_date = "2017-10-11"
```

This way you can't create dynamic date requests, bc there's a formar error. I've also tried using 

> actual_date = actual_date.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')

and it doesn't work neither.

Good luck, thank you!

issue_title: Management commands should inherit from BaseCommand (instead of NoArgsCommand)
issue_body:
Zinnia has two management commands that inherit from `NoArgsCommand`:

1. [spam_cleanup](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/management/commands/spam_cleanup.py#L10)
1. [count_discussions](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/management/commands/count_discussions.py#L10)

`NoArgsCommand` is deprecated since Django 1.8, and management commands should now inherit from `BaseCommand` instead according to [Django's documentation](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/howto/custom-management-commands/#django.core.management.NoArgsCommand).

issue_title: usage of thread local
issue_body:
Hi,

The [masterdb pinning](https://github.com/jbalogh/django-multidb-router/blob/master/multidb/pinning.py) uses a thread local. In Django since a thread may outlive a request and threads may be reused between requests, there is a lack of thread safety and threadlocals are not recommended for use.

For the masterdb pinning, this may mean that queries which can query standby servers may instead query the master, and queries which should only query the master may query slaves.

I didn't find a disclaimer regarding this. I was wondering what sort of guarantees does multidb router provide?

Thanks

issue_title: Recursive glob syntax support

The [`glob` module](https://docs.python.org/3/library/glob.html#glob.glob) support recursive wildcards. Support for this would be really useful to ignore specific types of errors, e.g. to ignore unused imports in `__init__.py` files. An example would look something like this:

```cfg
# setup.cfg
[flake8]
per-file-ignores =

```

issue_title: Unit tests should not execute Python subprocess
issue_body:
The unit test suite currently executes a Python interpreter in a sub-process. This should not be necessary, as the functionality to be tested is available by just calling a function — and this makes for a more tightly-defined test case.

Coupling the narrowly-defined test cases to a whole subprocess is fragile, and results in issues like issue #98.

Instead, the test cases should each invoke just a function in the ‘xkcd_password’ library, and test the result of calling that function.

issue_title: ConciseContract does not allow interfacing with methods through standard API
issue_body:
* Version: 0.3.x and 0.4.x


### What was wrong?

```python
# This works:
print(gnt.balanceOf(GNT_ADDR))
# This does not:
print(gnt.call({'from': web3.eth.coinbase}).balanceOf(GNT_ADDR))
```

The later produces this error:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./check.py", line 20, in <module>
    print(gnt.call({'from': web3.eth.coinbase}).balanceOf(GNT_ADDR))
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/contract.py", line 805, in __call__
    return self.__prepared_function(**kwargs)(*args)
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/contract.py", line 834, in call_contract_function
    transaction=transaction,
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/utils/decorators.py", line 17, in _wrapper
    return self.method(obj, *args, **kwargs)
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/contract.py", line 695, in _prepare_transaction
    fn_kwargs,
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/eth_utils/string.py", line 85, in inner
    return force_obj_to_text(fn(*args, **kwargs))
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/contract.py", line 734, in _encode_transaction_data
    fn_name, args, kwargs,
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/contract.py", line 663, in _get_function_info
    fn_abi = cls._find_matching_fn_abi(fn_name, args, kwargs)
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/contract.py", line 627, in _find_matching_fn_abi
    raise ValueError("No matching functions found")
ValueError: No matching functions found
```

The error occurs because `ConciseContract` tries to lookup a function named `call`.

### How can it be fixed?

The `ConciseContract` class should be augmented such that the `__getattr__` method will return the existing attribute on the contract class if present, only falling back to the contract method if no attribute by the requested name exists.

issue_title: Switch to `ctoolz` library.
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The `toolz` library is pretty awesome, but it is all pure python and thus, as slow as other pure python *stuff*.

Turns out there is a c implementation of the entire library!  https://pypi.python.org/pypi/cytoolz


### How can it be fixed?

Change to use [`ctoolz`](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/cytoolz)

issue_title: Make level of caching tunable when creating MatrixClient object
issue_body:
With #142 merged, in addition to keeping track of all rooms that the user is
currently in and their names, topics, aliases, etc., we also keep track of the
users in each room. This is very desirable for full-blown clients but
unnecessary overhead for some bots.

Before releasing next version, we should add kwarg allowing all caching to be
turned off completely to make memory usage low for purely "reactive" bots.

issue_title: Hurry mode for customer alerts is never True
issue_body:
[The code](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/62014cf6e36f5e1e612adc47dcbd7f859db3be4b/src/oscar/apps/customer/alerts/utils.py#L68-L77) to determine if hurry mode is set for a customer alert is checking that the number of active alerts are _less_ than the total number of items in stock for a product. 

    if num_stockrecords == 1:
        num_in_stock = stockrecords[0].num_in_stock
        # hurry_mode is false if num_in_stock is None
        hurry_mode = num_in_stock is not None and num_alerts < num_in_stock
    else:
        result = stockrecords.aggregate(max_in_stock=Max('num_in_stock'))
        hurry_mode = result['max_in_stock'] is not None and \
            num_alerts < result['max_in_stock']

I think it should be the reverse - check if the number of active alerts for a product are _greater_ than the number in stock. I'll submit a pull request to fix this.

issue_title: csvsql fails with misleading error if query and dialect are specified with no connection_string
issue_body:
**Behavior**
If I try to run csvsql with "dialect", "query" and no "connection_string", it will fail with a "The --dialect option is only valid when --db is not specified."

**Cause:**
Given that both "query" and "dialect" are being used, the run enters the following block which sets a default connection_string

```
# Create an SQLite database in memory if no connection string is specified```
        if query and not connection_string:
            connection_string = "sqlite:///:memory:"
            do_insert = True```
```
Then, the following block will always raise the error as there is now a connection string and a dialect:

```
if self.args.dialect and connection_string:
            self.argparser.error('The --dialect option is only valid when --db is not specified.')
```
**Suggestion:**
Refactor the code (csvsql.py). 
One quick possibility is to call the block that raises the error before the block that sets the default connection string.

We use the following pytest fixture for our tests:

```
@pytest.fixture(scope='function')
def app(request):

    wtm._patch_settings()
    request.addfinalizer(wtm._unpatch_settings)
    return django_webtest.DjangoTestApp()
```

In a test function, I'm trying to set something in the session:

```
def test_something(app):
    app.session['key'] = value

    # Now GET a URL which uses the session
    response = app.get(...)
```

The view never sees the `key` in its `self.request.session`.

In fact, in the test function itself, right after the `app.session['key'] = value` line, `app.session` doesn't contain the just set `key`.

It seems every time we call `app.session`, a new `django.contrib.sessions.backends.db.SessionStore` is instanciated, so nothing persists in it.

What am I missing? 😃

issue_title: Potentially redundant bias in convLSTM
issue_body:
https://github.com/deepmind/sonnet/blob/7da90b8e63fb684b91a12e1e9a42999962804e3a/sonnet/python/modules/gated_rnn.py#L1129

Hi, I realized that the variable of convLSTM includes two biases if `use_bias` is set to true. And I think it is because here the convolution is performed separately on input and state. But effectively only one set of bias is necessary. Perhaps adding bias at the end instead of within the convolution as done in the tensorflow contrib version (https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/pull/8891/files) would be better.

issue_title: Proposal: Deis Connector - A way to connect between two Deis
issue_body:
We were thinking about a way secure and reliable to containers running in separated Deis installations (and probably in production) can comunicate with each other.
The idea begins because we have two Deis installation on each environment. We have a Deis "Web Facing" and a Deis "Private".
The Web Facing Deis only receive and send https connections and the Private Deis only receive https connections but he can send tcp/http connections to resources / services like MySQL, Redis, MongoDB and more.

The initial idea was:

{ **WEB** }  *---(https)->* [ **Deis Web Facing** ] *---(https)->* [ **Deis Private** ]  *---(tcp/http)->* **Resource**

But we think that we can modify this connection between the two Deis installations and use a "connector" based on the AWS IAM way, using a KEY/SECRET and a JSON allowing / denying the access. 

##### Example of Deis Connector:
 - Frontend (Web Facing)
 - API (Private)
 - MySQL (Recurso)
 - DeisConnectorKey:  ``80MNOUQADpSu``
 - DeisConnectorPass: ``4QWU8f4ei1pnKEfyPN9Sr36X``
 - DeisConnectorJSON Example:

```json
{
	"DeisAuthorizations": [
		{
			"Action": "Access",
			"Effect": "Allow",
			"Rules": [
				{
					"Origin": "web-app.paas.comp.org",
					"Destination": "api-app.paas.com.org",
					"ConnectionType": "http"
				}
			]
		}
	]
}
```

With this, we can control between a module/app of own Deis the communication of external containers that belong to the same "Stack". Its very helpful in a environment like production where we want to limit the access.

issue_title: InsecurePlatformWarning during builds
issue_body:
Recently urllib3 began [emitting these warnings](https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html#insecureplatformwarning) during our normal builds whenever python touches SSL. Looks like we can fix it by updating to Python 2.7.9 (or by explicitly silencing it). An example:
```
Processing triggers for libc-bin (2.19-0ubuntu6.6) ...
/tmp/tmp8K2oTh/pip.zip/pip/_vendor/requests/packages/urllib3/util/ssl_.py:79: InsecurePlatformWarning: 
A true SSLContext object is not available. This prevents urllib3 from configuring SSL appropriately and 
may cause certain SSL connections to fail. For more information, see 
https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html#insecureplatformwarning.
```

issue_title: Use "release candidate" builds
issue_body:
For mostly historical reasons, the Deis release process begins with an intended final build (v1.11.0, let's say) rather than a release candidate build (v1.11.0-rc1 perhaps). This build may be updated inline once or more during final testing as showstopper bugs are found.

Instead, the Deis project could produce more traditional release candidate builds and make them generally available, which would allow the community to help with testing as well. A smoke test on multiple providers must pass cleanly before the release candidate is publicized for testing.

Caveats here are:
- Need to ensure that upgrade testing will work smoothly with v1.11.0-rc1 - type tags
- The project tags are clean and useful now--can we avoid pushing a `git` tag of "v1.11.0-rc1", or can we delete it afterward safely?
- `deisctl` is intimately tied to git tags and needs a custom -rc1 release
- versions are splattered throughout the codebase so each release candidate will require `bumpver` work

issue_title: How use LightFM for a session-based case?
issue_body:
I have this problem where we cannot track users and we only work at the session-level. So, each new session is assumed to be a new user. Given a session that has progressed to some level (some items have already been visited in that session), I wanted to predict the other interesting items for that session. I thought I could just assign a new user-id to each session and build the interaction matrix from the training data (it will probably be a sparse one). I also did not use any user/item features yet. But then at prediction, given a new session I was confused how to proceed given it will be a new user-id not seen in the training data. It will not be exactly a cold-start user problem as the session has some data and we know some of the interacted items for that session. The only problem is how to represent the new session using the previously seen item in the training. Is there any way to represent them in the user-features like the answer you gave here:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/46924119/lightfm-handling-user-and-item-cold-start

Or should I build the interaction matrix in the training considering the initial part of the test sessions as well? like if session s has n items, and we want to give predictions for item n+1, we should include session s with its n items in the training as well?

Thanks
