issue_title: Precompiled binaries for easier installation?
issue_body:
Our specific use case is that we are using lz4framed inside Docker containers.    The issue is that base linux in docker used for python often doesn't have the needed gcc components.   We use the miniconda3 docker base image and of the dozens of python packages we use (many of which require compilation), lz4framed is the only one that needs to have  gcc installed into the container.   

We can obviously apt-get install gcc, but having lz4framed packaged up into binaries would be a great benefit to its users like us.  I don't know what the compile dependencies are and if they impact wheels for pip, but if so conda can be used to build binaries so this isn't an issue and the package just installs anywhere easily.

issue_title: Use consistent naming for functions in letsencrypt-auto
issue_body: With the changes made in #4970, I believe all functions use CamelCase as their naming convention. The one exception to this are the single word `say` and `error` functions. They should be renamed to `Say` and `Error` for consistency.

issue_title: HTTP responses logged as hard-to-read bytes repr in Python 3
issue_body:
If you're having trouble using Certbot and aren't sure you've found a bug or
request for a new feature, please first try asking for help at
https://community.letsencrypt.org/. There is a much larger community there of
people familiar with the project who will be able to more quickly answer your
questions.

## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 16.04 (x86-64)

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
certbot-auto (Python 2) and the PPA (0.22.2, Python 3) on different systems.

## I ran this command and it produced this output:


## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
Under Python 2, Certbot logs HTTP responses as easy-to-read text, e.g.:

    2018-05-02 13:41:58,328:DEBUG:acme.client:Received response:
    HTTP 200
    Server: nginx
    Content-Type: application/json
    Content-Length: 724
    X-Frame-Options: DENY
    Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=604800
    Expires: Wed, 02 May 2018 13:41:58 GMT
    Cache-Control: max-age=0, no-cache, no-store
    Pragma: no-cache
    Date: Wed, 02 May 2018 13:41:58 GMT
    Connection: keep-alive

    {
      "6ELa2lV28v0": "https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/adding-random-entries-to-the-directory/33417",
      "keyChange": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/key-change",
      "meta": {
        "caaIdentities": [
          "letsencrypt.org"
        ],
        "termsOfService": "https://letsencrypt.org/documents/LE-SA-v1.2-November-15-2017.pdf",
        "website": "https://letsencrypt.org/docs/staging-environment/"
      },
      "newAccount": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/new-acct",
      "newNonce": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/new-nonce",
      "newOrder": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/new-order",
      "revokeCert": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/revoke-cert"
    }

Under Python 3, it logs the `repr()` of the bytes:

    2018-05-02 13:44:24,936:DEBUG:acme.client:Received response:
    HTTP 200
    Server: nginx
    Content-Type: application/json
    Content-Length: 724
    X-Frame-Options: DENY
    Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=604800
    Expires: Wed, 02 May 2018 13:44:24 GMT
    Cache-Control: max-age=0, no-cache, no-store
    Pragma: no-cache
    Date: Wed, 02 May 2018 13:44:24 GMT
    Connection: keep-alive
    
    b'{\n  "keyChange": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/key-change",\n  "meta": {\n    "caaIdentities": [\n      "letsencrypt.org"\n    ],\n    "termsOfService": "https://letsencrypt.org/documents/LE-SA-v1.2-November-15-2017.pdf",\n    "website": "https://letsencrypt.org/docs/staging-environment/"\n  },\n  "newAccount": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/new-acct",\n  "newNonce": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/new-nonce",\n  "newOrder": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/new-order",\n  "revokeCert": "https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/revoke-cert",\n  "uog1GW6DtvQ": "https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/adding-random-entries-to-the-directory/33417"\n}'

It makes log files more difficult to read.

Ideally Certbot would try to decode the HTTP response and log it normally. (Or perhaps dump the raw bytes straight to the log file.)

This affects JSON responses and certificates, but a certificate's worth of base64 isn't human readable anyway.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):

with that all being said, how can i stop the certbot client from trying the sites i no longer need?

issue_title: Fix changelog
issue_body: It should be included in the release and CHANGES.rst should probably be deleted.

issue_title: Enable EPEL in RPM bootstrapper
issue_body: [Some letsencrypt-auto runs on CentOS fail](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/help-test-the-new-letsencrypt-auto-script/8544/4) due to the EPEL repository being disabled, which [is the default for CentOS and perhaps others](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/help-test-the-new-letsencrypt-auto-script/8544/4). We should probably enable EPEL in our bootstrap script, since we enable Debian Backports in our Debian one.

issue_title: URIRef comparison with Python `str` fails
issue_body:
URIRef comparison with Python `str` fails in both Python v2 and v3.  
Per-char comparison of URIRef with str and `==` yield distinct values:
```python
import rdflib
from rdflib.namespace import RDF
typeProp = '<' + RDF.type + '>'  # N3/quad type property
prop = '<http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type>'

eq1 = typeProp == prop  # False
eq2 = len(prop) == len(typeProp) and not prop.find(typeProp)
assert eq1 == eq2, '{} vs {}'.format(eq1, eq2)
```
yields:
```sh
>>> assert eq1 == eq2, '{} vs {}'.format(eq1, eq2)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AssertionError: False vs True
```
Per-char comparison yields the same as inclusion search, but distinct from the equality check, which is confusing:
```python
class Entity(object):
	"""Entity to load RDF tripples"""
	# ATTENTION: type(RDF.type + 'x') is <class 'rdflib.term.URIRef'>, which breaks the str comparison
	#typeProp = '<' + str(RDF.type) + '>'  # N3/quad type property          OK
	typeProp = '<' + RDF.type + '>'  # FAILS str comparison
...
...
            prop = '<http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type>'
            print('extend(), props: \n{}\n{}\neq: {}'.format(prop, Entity.typeProp, prop == Entity.typeProp))
            for i in range(min(len(prop), len(Entity.typeProp))):
                if prop[i] != Entity.typeProp[i]:
                    print('Unequal pos: {}, val: {} ({}) vs {} ({})', i, prop[i], ord(prop[i]), Entity.typeProp[i], ord(Entity.typeProp[i]))
                    break
            else:
                print('len: {} vs {}, eq: {}, index: {}'.format(len(prop), len(Entity.typeProp), prop == Entity.typeProp,
                    prop.find(Entity.typeProp)))
```
yields:
```sh
extend(), props: 
<http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type>
<http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type>
eq: False
len: 49 vs 49, eq: False, index: 0
```
Investigating the cause:
```sh
>>> type(prop)
<type 'str'>
>>> type(typeProp)
<class 'rdflib.term.URIRef'>
```

The workaround is to perform the explicit type casting `typeProp = '<' + str(RDF.type) + '>' `, but this is not the appropriate solution for any scripting language. If `rdflib.term.URIRef` performs implicit casting from `str`, then it should also support at least `str` equality comparison operations in proper way.

issue_title: Add iPython to project_template requirements.txt
异步企业号接口
Possible wrong behavior of NCL
issue_body:
Following lines of **NeighbourhoodCleaningRule** class contains bug (IF statement):
   
    idx_to_exclude = []
    # Loop over the other classes under picking at random
    for key in self.stats_c_.keys():

        # Get the sample of the current class
        sub_samples_x = X[y == key]

        # Get the samples associated
        idx_sub_sample = np.flatnonzero(y == key)

        # Find the NN for the current class
        nnhood_idx = self.nn_.kneighbors(
            sub_samples_x, return_distance=False)

        # Get the label of the corresponding to the index
        nnhood_label = (y[nnhood_idx] == key)

        # Check which one are the same label than the current class
        # Make an AND operation through the three neighbours
        nnhood_bool = np.logical_not(np.all(nnhood_label, axis=1))

        # If the minority class remove the majority samples
        if key == self.min_c_:
            # Get the index to exclude
            idx_to_exclude += nnhood_idx[np.nonzero(nnhood_label[
                np.flatnonzero(nnhood_bool)])].tolist()
        else:
            # Get the index to exclude
            idx_to_exclude += idx_sub_sample[np.nonzero(
                nnhood_bool)].tolist()

As was mentioned in original article **"Improving Identification of Difficult Small Classes by Balancing Class Distribution"** by Jorma Laurikkala, having

    key = self.min_c_
should give as all negative neighbours of positive samples that misclassify these positive samples (suggesting minority class is positive).
But I runned the following code:
    
    from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors
    import numpy as np

    key = True
    X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [2, 0], [2, 1], [6, 6]])
    y = np.array([True, False, False, False, True])

    neigh = NearestNeighbors(3)
    neigh.fit(X)

    # Get the sample of the current class
    sub_samples_x = X[y == key]
    print('sub_samples_x:\n{}\n'.format(sub_samples_x))

    # Get the samples associated
    idx_sub_sample = np.flatnonzero(y == key)
    print('idx_sub_sample: {}\n'.format(idx_sub_sample))

    # Find the NN for the current class
    nnhood_idx = neigh.kneighbors(sub_samples_x, return_distance=False)
    print('nnhood_idx:\n{}\n'.format(nnhood_idx))

    # Get the label of the corresponding to the index
    nnhood_label = (y[nnhood_idx] == key)
    print('nnhood_label:\n{}\n'.format(nnhood_label))

    # Check which one are the same label than the current class
    # Make an AND operation through the three neighbours
    nnhood_bool = np.logical_not(np.all(nnhood_label, axis=1))
    print('nnhood_bool: {}\n'.format(nnhood_bool))

    # If the minority class remove the majority samples
    # IT IS MINORITY CLASS BECAUSE key=True
    print('idx_to_exclude: {}'.format(nnhood_idx[np.nonzero(nnhood_label[np.flatnonzero(nnhood_bool)])].tolist()))

Result:

    sub_samples_x:
    [[0 0]
     [6 6]]

    idx_sub_sample: [0 4]

    nnhood_idx:
    [[0 1 2]
     [4 3 2]]

    nnhood_label:
    [[ True False False]
     [ True False False]]

    nnhood_bool: [ True  True]

    idx_to_exclude: [0, 4]

The point is that for misclassified positive samples they are returned themselves but not their negative neighbours (so behaviour for positive result of IF statement is wrong).
However in the further code there positive sample idx doesn't play any role because all positive samples are saved.

I think that IF statement should look smth like that:
   
    # If the minority class remove the majority samples
    if key == self.min_c_:
        # Get the index to exclude
        idx_to_exclude += nnhood_idx[np.nonzero(~nnhood_label[np.flatnonzero(nnhood_bool)])].tolist()
    else:
        # Get the index to exclude
        idx_to_exclude += idx_sub_sample[np.nonzero(
            nnhood_bool)].tolist()

Correct me if i'm wrong and do not get smth.

issue_title: Gluon-based image-classification failed to run with dummy data on CPU machine when set as "symbolic" mode

i.e., when the command is like below:
